title: How White Blood Cells Hunt Down Infections
summary: White blood cells hunt infections by sensing chemical trails, leaving the bloodstream, engulfing microbes, killing infected cells, coordinating signals, and building immune memory.

lesson.learn: White blood cells are mobile defenders with different jobs. Neutrophils are rapid responders that follow chemical signals into infected tissue, engulf microbes, release antimicrobial granules, and sometimes cast DNA-based traps. Monocytes can become macrophages that swallow debris, digest pathogens, and help shape inflammation. Dendritic cells collect antigen evidence and carry it to lymph nodes. Natural killer cells inspect stressed or infected cells and can trigger their death. T cells recognize infected cells or coordinate other immune cells, while B cells become antibody-producing plasma cells. Hunting an infection is therefore not a single chase. It is a coordinated search using chemical gradients, adhesion molecules, tissue entry, receptor recognition, and communication. Each cell type sees a different part of the problem, and the immune response works when those views are integrated.
lesson.simpler: White blood cells find infections by following chemical signals, entering tissues, attacking microbes or infected cells, and coordinating with other immune cells.
lesson.keyTakeaway: White blood cells hunt infections through specialized teamwork: some arrive fast and engulf threats, some kill infected cells, and others coordinate targeted memory responses.
lesson.deeperDive: The journey from blood to infection site is tightly controlled. Inflammation makes nearby blood vessels express adhesion molecules, causing leukocytes to slow, roll, stick, and squeeze between endothelial cells in a process called extravasation. Chemokines then guide them toward higher concentrations of danger signals. Neutrophils dominate many early bacterial responses, while macrophages clear material and release signals that shape repair. Natural killer cells are especially important early in some viral infections because they detect cells with abnormal stress signals or reduced MHC-I display. Adaptive lymphocytes take longer to expand but can deliver extraordinary specificity. Cytotoxic T cells kill infected host cells, helper T cells coordinate responses, and B cells produce antibodies that neutralize or tag targets. White blood cells can also cause harm if they overreact; pus, swelling, tissue damage, and sepsis all reflect defense mechanisms pushed too far. Effective immunity depends on finding the target and knowing when to stop.
lesson.funFact: Pus is made largely of dead neutrophils, microbes, tissue debris, and inflammatory fluid.

people: Elie Metchnikoff - discovered phagocytosis as a central immune defense
Paul Ehrlich - helped classify blood cells and immune concepts
Ralph Steinman - discovered dendritic cells and their immune role
Hematologists - physicians and scientists who study blood cells
places: Bloodstream - transport route for circulating white blood cells
Endothelium - blood-vessel lining crossed during inflammation
Infected tissue - target site for recruited immune cells
Bone marrow - production site for many white blood cells
years: 1908 - Nobel Prize recognizes work on immunity by Metchnikoff and Ehrlich
1973 - dendritic cells are identified by Steinman and Cohn
Present day - live imaging reveals immune-cell movement in tissues
concepts: Extravasation - white blood cells leaving blood vessels
Natural killer cell - innate cell that kills stressed or infected cells
NETosis - neutrophil release of DNA traps
